blocks.content: They ate on the back porch because it was the shaded side, and the back porch had the box fan that moved the air enough to make it bearable. Mrs. Brooks sat with them for a while, asking each of them the same questions she always asked — how was the summer going, were they reading anything, had they been to the pool yet.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: Not the pool.

blocks.speaker: Mrs. Brooks
blocks.content: You should go to the pool.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: The pool's real crowded.

blocks.speaker: Mrs. Brooks
blocks.content: That's because it's summer.

blocks.speaker: Eli
blocks.content: Yeah. That's the problem.
She gave him the look. He accepted it.

Kit ate two sandwiches, which Mrs. Brooks noticed and said nothing about in a deliberate way — the kind of not-saying-something that was itself a thing. He'd been eating at Savannah's or Daisy's since he was nine, often enough that the courtesy of it had long since worn through into just a fact of the world, the way the utility sink was a fact.
Daisy sat beside him, close enough that their elbows were almost touching. She'd eaten half a sandwich and was picking at the second half. Kit glanced at the plate.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: What.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Nothing.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: You're thinking.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: People think. That's allowed.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: You do the thing with your sandwich when you're thinking.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: I do not do a thing.

blocks.speaker: Kit
blocks.content: You pull the crusts apart.
She looked at the plate. Four separate crust pieces, arranged around the edges.
She reassembled the sandwich with a certain dignity.

blocks.speaker: Daisy
blocks.content: Maybe I like the crusts separate.
Mrs. Brooks, from her end of the porch, said nothing. But her shoulders moved slightly.
